fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, following up on the distributed tracing initiative we discussed last sprint. We need to build something that basically wraps all the usual stuff our microservice framework does — handling web requests, talking to downstream services, hitting the database, running things in the background, the scheduler stuff, the message bus, and the circuit breaker thing — and automatically produces tracing data without developers having to touch their business logic at all. Think of it like the logging approach we used for that auth module rollout: plug it in, it just works.

The big pain point from ops right now is that when something goes wrong in production, we have no visibility into which hop in the chain failed or where the latency is coming from. Support tickets are piling up because engineers have to manually grep logs across five services to piece together what happened.

We also need a way to verify this works — some kind of test runner that can go through a directory of test scenarios and tell us pass/fail. The output format needs to be totally deterministic so we can do automated checking, no random IDs or anything environment-specific leaking into the results. There are also some edge cases around skipping certain paths and handling unreachable backends that need to be covered. The rendering for the gateway and message channel scenarios is slightly different from the others — check how we did something similar before for the format details.

trigger_keywords: output format for spans
how should finished spans be rendered
what does the text output look like for a span
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Each span line must follow this exact shape: `op=<operation name> component=<component|-> span.kind=<kind|-> http.status=<status|-> attrs=[k=v;...sorted] error=<true|false> error_log=<event|-> parent=<parent operation|root|external>`. The full output block starts with `spans=<total>`, then `distinct_traces=<number>`, then one sorted line per span. When there are no spans, distinct_traces should be 0. Parent is `root` when there is no parent span, and `external` when the parent exists but is not part of the recorded set.

trigger_keywords: gateway output format
how is the gateway scenario rendered differently
what does the edge gateway result look like
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The gateway feature uses a purpose-specific rendering: total span count, distinct traces count, per-component span counts (sorted, formatted as `component.<name>=<count>`), and then — when a gateway span exists — `gateway.op=<method>`, `gateway.http.status=<code>`, `gateway.route.host=<url>`, `gateway.error=<true|false>`. This is different from the standard per-span line format used by other features.

trigger_keywords: message channel output format
how is websocket or pub-sub rendered
what fields does the message channel result include
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The message channel feature uses its own summary format: `op=<destination>`, `component=websocket`, `span.kind=<server|client>`, `parent_linked=<true|false>`, `same_trace_as_parent=<true|false>`, and for the outbound (client) case also `context_propagated=<true|false>`. It does not use the standard span-list rendering.

trigger_keywords: skip pattern for HTTP
which paths are suppressed
how does path filtering work for server spans
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A configured skip pattern suppresses the server span for matching paths, but outbound client spans are still produced. In the test cases, `/notTraced` matches the skip pattern so the server span is omitted, while a `rest_client` call to that path still yields one client span. The path `/hello` is traced normally (no suppression).

trigger_keywords: test harness command line options
how does the test runner work
what arguments does test.sh accept
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The test harness is at `rcb_tests/test.sh`. It accepts a `--cases-dir <subdir>` option (defaulting to `test_cases`). It runs every case in the chosen directory. Raw standard output for each invocation is written to `rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/<file-stem>@<NNN>.txt` where NNN is the zero-padded three-digit case index. It reports a pass/total summary at the end.

trigger_keywords: test case directories
where are the test cases stored
full contract suite vs public cases
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: There are two case directories: `rcb_tests/test_cases/` contains the full contract suite, and `rcb_tests/public_test_cases/` contains the representative cases embedded in the PRD document. The test harness should support running either via the `--cases-dir` option.

trigger_keywords: how are span identifiers handled in output
should trace IDs appear in output
determinism of output across runs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Span and trace identifiers must never appear in the rendered output. Parent links are rendered by the parent's operation name, not by ID. Span lines are emitted in a stable sorted order. The number of distinct traces is reported as a count, not as raw trace IDs. This ensures the output is fully deterministic across runs and environments.

trigger_keywords: error recording on spans
how is a failure represented in span output
what does an error span look like
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Failures are surfaced as `error=true` on the relevant span and a neutral `error_log=error` event (not a host-language exception type or backend-specific string). Successful spans have `error=false` and `error_log=-`. For example, an async method that throws produces: `error=true error_log=error`.

trigger_keywords: how does context propagate across threads
executor context propagation
how does the circuit breaker keep trace context
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The currently active span is treated as the parent of any span started while it is active. Context must survive executor hand-offs, asynchronous results, and serialization into/out of message carriers. For managed executors and circuit-breaker worker threads, the instrumentation layer wraps submitted work to capture and restore the calling thread's active span context, so worker-created spans become children of the caller span.

trigger_keywords: what HTTP client components are supported
rest template vs async rest template
browser caller vs instrumented client
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Three caller types are defined: `browser` (external, non-instrumented — produces no client span, only the server side may be traced), `rest_client` (instrumented synchronous RestTemplate — produces a client span with component `java-spring-rest-template`), and `async_rest_client` (instrumented asynchronous client — also produces a client span). The server span component is `java-web-servlet`.

trigger_keywords: database span attributes
what tags does a JDBC span carry
which fields appear on a database call span
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A database span has: `op=Execute`, `component=java-jdbc`, `span.kind=client`, and attrs containing `db.statement=<statement text>`, `db.type=h2`, and `db.user=SA` (sorted). HTTP status is `-` since it is not an HTTP operation.

trigger_keywords: async background method span attributes
what tags does a scheduled or async span carry
class and method attributes on async spans
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Background async method spans use `component=async` and carry `class=<SimpleClassName>` and `method=<methodName>` in attrs. Scheduled task spans use `component=scheduled` and also carry `class` and `method` attrs. For example: `op=runJob component=async span.kind=- http.status=- attrs=[class=BackgroundJobs;method=runJob]`.

trigger_keywords: gateway port numbers in test
what ports does the gateway test use
route host URL format for gateway cases
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: In the gateway test cases, the successful route (`/test`) resolves to `http://localhost:53751/hello` and the unreachable backend (`/wrong-port`) resolves to `http://localhost:53752/wrong-port`. These specific port numbers and paths appear in the `gateway.route.host` field of the rendered output.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: Just refer to the compatibility logic from that login module we did last time
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The ambient activation pattern described in the PRD architecture constraints: the instrumentation layer activates only when a tracer is available on the classpath and otherwise stays inert, without requiring changes to business code beyond ordinary framework configuration.

context_pointers.vague_reference: The rendering for the gateway and message channel scenarios is slightly different from the others — check how we did something similar before for the format details
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Gateway feature uses the purpose-specific summary format (component counts + gateway.* fields) and message channel uses its own summary (op, component, span.kind, parent_linked, same_trace_as_parent, context_propagated), both defined in Features 2 and 8 of start.md respectively, distinct from the standard per-span line format used by features 1, 3, 4, 5, 6, and 7.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect handy — you'll want to dig into the existing codebase and PRD for the exact spec. The key thing from the business side is that the output must be completely deterministic and human-checkable, failures should be clearly flagged, and the whole thing should be transparent to the application developer.

feature1_http_request.json.description: HTTP server and client span instrumentation, including skip-pattern handling.

cases.name: instrumented_client_creates_client_and_server_spans
cases.expected_output:
spans=2
distinct_traces=1
op=GET component=java-spring-rest-template span.kind=client http.status=200 attrs=[http.method=GET;http.url=http://localhost/hello] error=false error_log=- parent=root
op=hello component=java-web-servlet span.kind=server http.status=200 attrs=[http.method=GET;http.url=http://localhost/hello] error=false error_log=- parent=GET

cases.name: skip_pattern_suppresses_server_span_only
cases.expected_output:
spans=1
distinct_traces=1
op=GET component=java-spring-rest-template span.kind=client http.status=200 attrs=[http.method=GET;http.url=http://localhost/notTraced] error=false error_log=- parent=root

feature2_gateway_route.json.description: Edge-gateway (reverse proxy) routing spans: route host, downstream status, error recording, and non-routed pass-through.

cases.name: successful_route_records_status_and_route_host
cases.expected_output:
spans=3
distinct_traces=1
component.java-web-servlet=2
component.zuul=1
gateway.op=GET
gateway.http.status=200
gateway.route.host=http://localhost:53751/hello
gateway.error=false

cases.name: unreachable_downstream_records_500_and_error
cases.expected_output:
spans=2
distinct_traces=1
component.java-web-servlet=1
component.zuul=1
gateway.op=GET
gateway.http.status=500
gateway.route.host=http://localhost:53752/wrong-port
gateway.error=true

feature3_database_call.json.description: Database call instrumentation: acquiring a connection and executing a statement is traced; the span correlates with the active caller span when present.

cases.name: raw_connection_statement_is_traced
cases.expected_output:
spans=1
distinct_traces=1
op=Execute component=java-jdbc span.kind=client http.status=- attrs=[db.statement=select 1;db.type=h2;db.user=SA] error=false error_log=- parent=root

cases.name: statement_span_is_child_of_active_caller
cases.expected_output:
spans=2
distinct_traces=1
op=Execute component=java-jdbc span.kind=client http.status=- attrs=[db.statement=select 1;db.type=h2;db.user=SA] error=false error_log=- parent=caller
op=caller component=- span.kind=- http.status=- attrs=[] error=false error_log=- parent=root

feature4_background_method.json.description: Background (asynchronous) method instrumentation: invocation produces a span tagged with the originating type and method; failures are recorded as an error.

cases.name: async_method_span_correlated_with_caller
cases.expected_output:
spans=3
distinct_traces=1
op=bar component=- span.kind=- http.status=- attrs=[] error=false error_log=- parent=root
op=inner-work component=- span.kind=- http.status=- attrs=[] error=false error_log=- parent=bar
op=runJob component=async span.kind=- http.status=- attrs=[class=BackgroundJobs;method=runJob] error=false error_log=- parent=bar

cases.name: async_method_failure_recorded_as_error
cases.expected_output:
spans=1
distinct_traces=1
op=failingJob component=async span.kind=- http.status=- attrs=[class=BackgroundJobs;method=failingJob] error=true error_log=error parent=root

feature5_executor_submit.json.description: Managed-executor instrumentation: work submitted to an executor keeps the caller's active trace context, so a span created inside the worker is correlated with the caller's span.

cases.name: thread_pool_executor_propagates_context
cases.expected_output:
spans=2
distinct_traces=1
op=caller component=- span.kind=- http.status=- attrs=[] error=false error_log=- parent=root
op=worker component=- span.kind=- http.status=- attrs=[] error=false error_log=- parent=caller

feature6_circuit_breaker.json.description: Circuit-breaker instrumentation: commands run on breaker worker threads keep the caller's trace context, fallback invocations are recorded, and a traced command wrapper tags the command's identifying metadata.

cases.name: command_runs_in_caller_trace
cases.expected_output:
spans=2
distinct_traces=1
op=caller component=- span.kind=- http.status=- attrs=[] error=false error_log=- parent=root
op=sayHello component=- span.kind=- http.status=- attrs=[] error=false error_log=- parent=caller

cases.name: fallback_invocation_is_recorded
cases.expected_output:
spans=3
distinct_traces=1
op=alwaysFail component=- span.kind=- http.status=- attrs=[] error=false error_log=- parent=caller
op=caller component=- span.kind=- http.status=- attrs=[] error=false error_log=- parent=root
op=defaultGreeting component=- span.kind=- http.status=- attrs=[fallback=yes] error=false error_log=- parent=caller

feature7_scheduled_task.json.description: Scheduled-task instrumentation: a method invoked by the scheduler is wrapped in a span tagged with the originating type and method, and any span created inside the task is correlated as its child.

cases.name: scheduled_invocation_traced_and_correlates_child
cases.expected_output:
spans=2
distinct_traces=1
op=child component=- span.kind=- http.status=- attrs=[] error=false error_log=- parent=reportMetrics
op=reportMetrics component=scheduled span.kind=- http.status=- attrs=[class=ScheduledJob;method=reportMetrics] error=false error_log=- parent=root

feature8_message_channel.json.description: Message-channel instrumentation for a publish/subscribe broker: a message flowing through the channel creates a span named after the destination, tagged with the channel component and span kind, linked to the surrounding trace (remote parent on server side, active span on client side, with context propagated onward).

cases.name: outbound_message_links_active_parent_and_propagates_context
cases.expected_output:
op=/topic/greetings
component=websocket
span.kind=client
parent_linked=true
same_trace_as_parent=true
context_propagated=true
